title: Editing, data sourcing, and imputation: Planned approach for the 2023 Census | Stats NZ
content:
## Editing, data sourcing, and imputation: Planned approach for the 2023 Census
28 September 2022, 1:00pm
Editing, data sourcing, and imputation: Planned approach for the 2023 Census summarises Stats NZ’s planned approach for detecting and resolving errors in the 2023 Census and for filling in gaps when people or dwelling characteristics have not been provided on census forms.
Details of the final implemented methodology will be published following the 2023 Census.
Download the PDF document and read the summary online.
- Editing data sourcing and imputation: Planned approach for the 2023 Census Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 367 KB

### Editing data sourcing and imputation: Planned approach for the 2023 Census
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 367 KB

## Summary of key points
Stats NZ applies edits to detect and resolve errors and uses statistical methods to deal with missing or otherwise invalid data in census responses. We follow these processes to improve the overall quality of census outputs.
The 2018 Census was the first in New Zealand to use data from admin and historical census sources to fill gaps in census attributes for individuals and dwellings. The use of statistical imputation was also extended in scope to cover a wider range of variables than in previous censuses.
For the 2023 Census, we intend to use similar approaches to the 2018 Census, with fixes as required to deliver a ‘combined census model by design’. We have split the approach into the three broad sections of:
- editing (to detect and resolve errors to improve data quality)
- using administrative (admin) data and historical (2013 and 2018) census responses
- using statistical imputation for item non-response, where no other data sources are available.
- automates edit checks and resolution as far as possible to resolve as many inconsistencies as possible and minimises manual intervention
- prioritises the use of 2023 Census responses where possible
- uses admin and historical census data and statistical imputation to minimise missing attributes in the census file and reduce possible bias from non-response
- prioritises the use of admin and historical census data over statistical imputation
- incorporates quality improvements from the 2018 Census where information needs or critical quality limitations are identified and it is ethical and appropriate to do so
- provides information for internal and external users about the sources, methods, and quality for each attribute
- emphasises early and ongoing consultation with key stakeholders around data sources and methods, including Māori and iwi and Rainbow groups.
This paper is one of a collection of documents summarising our approach to the combined census model design. Other papers to read include:
- Admin enumeration: Planned approach for the 2023 Census , which describes our planned methods for adding individuals to the census file from administrative (admin) data
- Family and household methodology: Planned approach for the 2023 Census , which describes our planned methods for family coding.
ISBN 978-1-99-104906-3 (online)
